title: How farmland is becoming the main driver of social-political conflict

chunks.title: Agricultural Toxins and Cancer Rates
chunks.summary: This segment discusses the significant correlation between agricultural runoff, particularly glyphosate, and cancer rates observed in Louisiana, also referred to as 'cancer alley.' The speaker highlights how the distribution of these toxins through water systems and air has played a crucial role in creating cancer hotspots, particularly after 1996.
chunks.transcript: all of that 80% sprayed happens to go into a single river system which is the Mississippi tributaries and so they start up near the border of of Canada and they collect that 80% of farm runoff from those environments in that any water soluble nutrient toxin herbicide pesticide whatever it is is going to end up in that water body and the Mississippi ends in the last 90 miles of it in Louisiana in the Gulf of Mexico there Louisiana uh has the highest cancer rates the entire developed world now as it collects that huge swath glyphosate we call that cancer alley so the cancer alley runs between bat marouge and New Orleans and so the highest rates of cancer in the developed world are sitting right there at our glyphosate you know epicenter and when we looked at the Cancer pattern around the country and superpose that you realize it's not the spraying of the chemical that's mapping our cancer it's the accumulation of it within water systems and its interpret it's then distribution through air and rain and so it's the air rain distribution of that Mississippi River that you can see our cancer hotspot became and the Striking thing that I was showing is that before 1996 our cancer in the United States was always as far back as we measured it over a 100 years had always been the Northeast with one little pocket in the Northwest around prostate cancer but cancers across the board were always in our hot pocket of the Northeast we had never seen a cancer epidemic in the deep south of the United States and this is very important because a lot of people will justify our current cancer map by being a map of socioeconomic risk well poor people and depressed economies and blah blah blah no mississippi Louisiana were some of our poorest states in 1995 and they are today and the cancer risk was not there in 1995 and by 2005 they had become the epicenter so it was not an economic phenomenon it is fully a

chunks.title: Global Responses to Glyphosate Use
chunks.summary: In this segment, the speaker addresses the global effort to combat glyphosate usage, citing examples from France, Russia, and Japan. The discussion emphasizes how these countries are pushing towards organic farming and the challenges posed by industry and special interest groups.
chunks.transcript: phenomenon wow so United States Midwest couple of pockets Southern California Washington Oregon Brazil China there are some places in Europe where they're sort of getting rid of it right like I for of it over there yet but they're they're making efforts towards it and so there's hopes that France will be maybe you know free of glyphosate by 2025 but every time they set the the guard post it or the guide post it they move because industry comes in and there's special interest push and the governments are having a really hard time like putting down their foot I believe pretty deeply that the first fully organic country in the world is going to be Russia they set goals um almost six years back that they would become a completely organic uh production system by 2025 and they already you know a long time back were the cleanest production system of grains and other legumes uh to the to the world so much so that by I think it's three to five years ago Germany jumped on board with Japan that had done it previously was to Outlaw the importation of all North American grains Canada and the United States both practicing these chemical intensive methods for producing corn soybean wheat and the rest and so grains uh grain importation got outlawed in in Japan and then subsequently in Germany and Germany uh then became their primary export or import uh locations uh were from Ukraine

chunks.title: Geopolitical Impact of Food Systems
chunks.summary: The speaker analyzes the shifting political landscape where food systems are increasingly becoming a focal point of conflict. This includes Ukraine's role in global agriculture and how the control of food sources has implications for economic power dynamics between nations.
chunks.transcript: current sociopolitical you know expression of War it used to just be follow the oil that that was how you mapped you know conflicts and and where Wars would be started now you can follow oil and soil and see exactly where the wars are being seated and so uh the Ukraine war sits at the breed basket of Germany and Europe and the United States is now buying up through Vanguard and black rock most of that uh that uh Growing System there towards the end of the Ukrainian conflict there and so I I really deeply believe that social political conflict is now seeing that economics is not just driven by oil it is driven by Food Systems ultimately and who controls the food system globally is

chunks.title: Historical Context of Food Production
chunks.summary: Here, the speaker outlines the historical relationship between warfare, colonization, and food production, illustrating how exploitation has shaped agricultural systems for centuries. This segment underscores the significance of land ownership and the dependency on external labor markets.
chunks.transcript: and so we're seeing a pivot from oil to soil as the most valuable asset on the planet and you can see the pivot of big you know foundations The Gates Foundation and things like that starting to buy up farmland and so if you want control of the future of of humanity it's going to have to do with Food Systems and so if as a Humanity we want to really pivot towards a future that's you know sustainable and has Freedom involved we're going to need to start to look at distributed Food Systems distributed models of of soil relationship uh for communities to start to you know have access to their own soil systems have access to their own food systems which seems kind of obvious but if you look at the United States for example our our average uh food supply chain is 3,000 kilometers so you know we we stopped growing food in our communities a very long time ago you know like 80 years ago and so we're way down the path of deep dependence on extraction models that are relying on slave labor kind of models where you get super cheap labor in you know Peru or other parts of South America and so America is exploiting that Europe has been exploiting Africa for decades now with their food system and so we have this colonialism slave labor kind of model towards food for all of history you look back thousands of years the Egyptians were fighting Wars to get slave labor to grow their food so it's

chunks.title: Sustainable Future & Social Responsibility
chunks.summary: In this segment, the focus shifts to the urgent need for humanity to recognize its relationship with food systems and nature. The speaker argues for a transition towards sustainable practices and the importance of community-based food production as a means of empowerment and resilience.
chunks.transcript: ownership of of soil now is kind of the Insidious overlay to that cheap economy of Labor that the food system has has been dominated by so we're at a deep Tipping Point not just you know so from a biologic standpoint I think we're finally getting a spiritual reality check here of humanity if you want to stick around you're you're going to have to find your Humanity you're going to have to discover that you are nature and nature speaking through you and this is your opportunity to become something new and so as dismal as all of these numbers sound and all of this reality sounds My Silver Lining that I wake up every day to and have the gumption to go ahead and put another foot forward and try to get these projects Global and everything else this reality that Humanity never changes until we have hit the wall that's the only thing that the human psyche can respond to to to

chunks.title: Philosophical Reflection on Humanity’s Future
chunks.summary: The segment concludes with a philosophical reflection on humanity's current crisis and the potential for transformation. The speaker suggests that only through facing challenges can a deeper self-realization and reconnection to nature emerge, promoting a spiritual journey towards a more indigenous and connected existence.
chunks.transcript: encourage and inight Total Transformation and we are in a 70,000 year end of a cycle here of of Humanity's expression over 300,000 year period the last 70,000 years of civilization is coming to an end and we need to reexpress our human qualities our human spirit in this next iteration going forward after this decade and so in 2030 who are we and how do we show up on the planet and I'm very excited that we're being taken to our knees at this moment so that can ask that question from a deep place of self-reflection and a deep space of realization that there is no monetary solution here there's no technological solution there's in some ways only a spiritual reconnection that has to happen as we realize ourselves to be indigenous to planet Earth and if we could remember that indigenous culture
